text: Missed the gym this morning for the third time this week. Planned to go after working on the column draft, but it didn't happen. Noticed a direct impact on sharpness and patience from skipping it. Not surprising, but important to note.

{"notes": "Missed gym for the third time this week. Noticed feeling sharper and less patient.", "date": "2026-05-18"}

Missed the gym again this morning, making it three times this week. I had the intention to go after working on the column, but it didn't materialize. I've observed a clear connection between skipping the gym and feeling sharper, but also notably less patient. This isn't a new discovery, but it's a pattern I need to pay more attention to.

Third gym miss this week. Told myself I'd go after the column draft and didn't. Notices the pattern clearly: sharper and less patient when the morning workout doesn't happen. "Should not be surprised by this."

Missed the gym this morning for the third time this week. I kept telling myself I'd go after finishing the column draft but it never happened. I've noticed I'm sharper but less patient when I skip the workout. I really shouldn't be surprised by this pattern anymore.

Self-aware about the mood/patience correlation \u2014 not surprised but still not solving for it.", "date": "2026-05-18", "missed_gym": true, "gym_misses_this_week": 3, "mood": "sharper, less patient", "focus": "column draft", "gym_missed_count": 3, "blocker": "Deferring gym until after writing work; never materializes"}

Third missed gym this week. The pattern is the same each time: defer until after the column draft, and it disappears. He noted it himself — sharper and less patient without it. The irony being that he knows the mechanism cold. The cortisol, the dopamine, the effect on prefrontal regulation. Knowing the study doesn't protect you from the behavior. He said "should not be surprised by this" — which has the sound of someone who has been surprised by this several times in a row.
